perturbation.original_text: The COMPANY and CELEBRITY may terminate this Agreement under the following conditions: (A) By the COMPANY. -------------- (i) If during the Term of this Agreement the CELEBRITY is unable to provide the Services as set forth herein for thirty (30) consecutive business days because of illness, accident, or other incapacity of CELEBRITY Personnel; or, (ii) If CELEBRITY willfully breaches or neglects the duties required to be performed hereunder; or, (B) By CELEBRITY ------------ (i) If the COMPANY fails to make any payments or provide information required hereunder; or, (ii) If the COMPANY ceases business or, other than in an Initial Merger, sells a controlling interest to a third party, or agrees to a consolidation or merger of itself with or into another corporation, or enters into such a transaction outside of the scope of this Agreement, or sells substantially all of its assets to another corporation, entity or individual outside of the scope of this Agreement; or, (iii) If the COMPANY subsequent to the execution hereof has a receiver appointed for its business or assets, or otherwise becomes insolvent or unable to timely satisfy its obligations in the ordinary course of, including but not limited to the obligation to pay the Initial Fee, the Transaction fee, or the CELEBRITY Fee; or,
perturbation.changed_text: The COMPANY and CELEBRITY may terminate this Agreement under the following conditions: (A) By the COMPANY. -------------- (i) If during the Term of this Agreement the CELEBRITY is unable to provide the Services as set forth herein for thirty (30) consecutive business days because of illness, accident, or other incapacity of CELEBRITY Personnel; or, (ii) If CELEBRITY willfully breaches or neglects the duties required to be performed hereunder; or, (B) By CELEBRITY ------------ (i) If the COMPANY fails to make any payments or provide information required hereunder; or, (ii) If the COMPANY ceases business or, other than in an Initial Merger, sells a controlling interest to a third party, or agrees to a consolidation or merger of itself with or into another corporation, or enters into such a transaction outside of the scope of this Agreement, or sells substantially all of its assets to another corporation, entity or individual outside of the scope of this Agreement; or, (iii) If the COMPANY subsequent to the execution hereof has a receiver appointed for its business or assets, or otherwise becomes insolvent.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "or unable to timely satisfy its obligations in the ordinary course of, including but not limited to the obligation to pay the Initial Fee, the Transaction fee, or the CELEBRITY Fee; or," obscures the celebrity's right to terminate in case of the company's failure to meet financial obligations. This can be detrimental for the celebrity and increases difficulty in determining whether breach of contract has occurred.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 104.2302 - Unconscionable contract or clause
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit mention of the company's failure to meet payment obligations as grounds for termination by the celebrity, the contract becomes potentially unconscionable under NRS 104.2302. This statute allows courts to refuse to enforce a contract if it finds the contract or any clause of the contract to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The modification makes the celebrity's right to terminate due to non-payment less clear, favoring the COMPANY unfairly.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit inclusion of 'the obligation to pay the Initial Fee, the Transaction fee, or the CELEBRITY Fee' as reasons for the celebrity to terminate the contract, which creates a structural flaw. The law explanation suggests this could be unconscionable under NRS 104.2302, potentially favoring the company unfairly. The Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes NRS 104.2302, 'Unconscionable contract or clause.' This supports the argument that omitting specific payment obligations as grounds for termination could lead to a finding of unconscionability, hence a legal contradiction because it obscures the celebrity's right to terminate for non-payment.

perturbation.original_text: The CELEBRITY will have full release of liability in regards to product performance and/or law suites resulting from use of the product. This liability will remain the responsibility of the COMPANY and manufacturers.
perturbation.changed_text: This liability will remain the responsibility of the COMPANY and manufacturers. The CELEBRITY will have full release of liability in regards to product performance and/or law suites resulting from use of the product.
perturbation.explanation: Swapping these two sentences, where the second sentence is dependent on the first, makes the provision regarding the CELEBRITY's liability less clear. While legally, this still implies they're not responsible, readers may interpret it incorrectly. This can affect enforceability, especially if the celebrity relies on the products and then a problem occurs.
perturbation.law_explanation: By inverting the sentences the clarity of the limited liability is reduced. This might violate the law about transparency requirements (NRS 597.170), because it is not disclosed in an appropriate manner.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a structural flaw that affects clarity, potentially violating transparency requirements akin to those in NRS 597.170, although that statute refers to alcoholic beverages. By inverting the sentences, the clear release of liability for the CELEBRITY is obscured. This reduces the enforceability, as the order in which the provision is read may be interpreted incorrectly. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
